costs: Political cost of visible contraction against organized director lobbying interests (F-live-political_feasibility-07)

Parental backlash risk given oversubscription already generates frustration; tighter thresholds may push more students into vocational tracks against family preference (F-live-school_network_planning-04)
Administrative cost of managing differentiated capacity across state vs. church/private sectors
content.equity_impact: Reduces the scale of the segregating mechanism system-wide but does not directly change who is selected within remaining places; may leave the most advantaged families with even more concentrated influence over fewer places unless paired with T1-style admission reform
expected_benefits: Reduces the absolute and relative size of the segregating mechanism without requiring a change to selection age or admissions criteria design
Uses discretionary tools already legally exercised and politically tested (F-live-hungarian_education_system-09)

Aligns with demographic trend, reducing political framing risk as the minister has already linked declining demographics to reduced justification for the current system (F-live-hungarian_education_system-10)
content.goal: Shrink the state-sector footprint of 6/8-year gimnázium places using score thresholds and capacity caps rather than admission-rule redesign

implementation_steps.action: Convert the 2024/25 recommended 70-point threshold from advisory guidance into binding national minimum for state-sector track admission
Freeze or reduce state-sector 6/8-year gimnázium capacity allocations in multi-year school-network plan, prioritizing reduction in oversubscribed urban flagship schools
Apply discretionary approval power systematically to deny new or expanded 6/8-year tracks in state and, where legally possible, church/foundation schools citing segregation risk
Review whether capacity caps require legislative backing versus administrative regulation, given Act CXC codification

mechanisms: Binding national minimum score thresholds (building on 2024/25 recommended 70-point threshold) applied uniformly to reduce places awarded below a rigor bar (F-live-school_network_planning-04)
Capacity caps on state-sector 6/8-year gimnázium enrollment, holding or reducing the current ~25,962 stagnant capacity in the face of falling cohorts (F-live-school_network_planning-03)
Use of demographic decline as political cover to justify contraction rather than framing as ideological reversal (F-live-hungarian_education_system-10)

Ministerial discretionary approval power already used to deny track expansion requests on segregation grounds, extended systematically (F-live-hungarian_education_system-09, F-live-political_feasibility-08)
risks: Church and foundation schools, outside Klebelsberg jurisdiction, could absorb displaced demand and expand their own selective tracks, undermining net contraction (F-live-legal_and_governance-07, F-live-implementation_planning-05)
Contraction without addressing admission-mechanism bias could simply concentrate remaining highly limited places even more among already-advantaged families
Risk of political reversal given documented director lobbying access to top leadership (F-live-political_feasibility-07)
content.title: Score-threshold and capacity-cap contraction of the selective state-sector track
